title.fr: Déployer un agent IA en entreprise
title.en: Deploying an Enterprise AI Agent Pilot

description.fr: Construire un plan de pilote 60 ou 90 jours pour un agent IA d'entreprise : périmètre, rôles, KPI, support, communication et critères de décision finale.
description.en: Build a 60-day or 90-day pilot plan for an enterprise AI agent: scope, roles, KPIs, support, communication, and final decision criteria.

title.fr: Prototype, pilote, production — ne pas confondre
title.en: Prototype, pilot, production — do not confuse them

content.fr:
Vous avez maintenant les briques essentielles : agent, workflow, outils, RAG, mémoire, architecture, sécurité, gouvernance, évaluation et observabilité.

Cette dernière leçon répond à la question opérationnelle :
> Comment passer d'un prototype agentique à un pilote réel dans l'organisation ?

Un agent d'entreprise ne réussit pas parce que la démonstration est impressionnante. Il réussit si les utilisateurs l'adoptent, si les risques sont maîtrisés, si les KPI montrent une valeur, si les équipes savent quoi faire en cas d'erreur.

### Les trois stades

**Prototype :** teste la faisabilité. L'agent lit quelques tickets exemples et prépare des réponses.

**Pilote :** teste la valeur et le risque dans un périmètre limité. L'agent assiste une équipe pendant 6 semaines, prépare des brouillons, crée des notes et escalade les cas sensibles.

**Production :** intègre l'agent dans l'organisation. Suivi par un dashboard, gouverné par un propriétaire métier, maintenu par l'équipe IT/IA.

Le passage de prototype à pilote demande un changement de logique : vous ne testez plus seulement la technologie. Vous testez le système humain, métier et technique.
content.en:
You now have the core building blocks: agent, workflow, tools, RAG, memory, architecture, security, governance, evaluation, and observability.

This final lesson answers the operational question:
> How do you move from an agentic prototype to a real pilot inside the organization?

An enterprise agent does not succeed because the demo is impressive. It succeeds if users adopt it, risks are controlled, KPIs show value, teams know what to do when errors appear.

### The three stages

**Prototype:** tests feasibility. The agent reads a few sample tickets and prepares responses.

**Pilot:** tests value and risk in a limited scope. The agent assists one team for 6 weeks, prepares drafts, creates notes, and escalates sensitive cases.

**Production:** integrates the agent into the organization. Monitored through a dashboard, governed by a business owner, maintained by the IT/AI team.

Moving from prototype to pilot changes the logic: you are no longer testing only technology. You are testing a human, business, and technical system.

checkpoint_config.topic: Designing a realistic pilot — scope, roles, progressive mode, and decision criteria
checkpoint_config.section_context: The learner read about good vs poor pilot scope, the 7 roles to define, the 4-step progressive deployment (observation → assistance → limited action → controlled extension), the 60-day and 90-day plan structures, and the 4 final decisions (continue, adjust, stop, scale). A pilot must refuse immediate wide deployment and start with observation mode.
checkpoint_config.hint: A valid answer refuses immediate wide deployment, proposes a limited and specific scope, defines at least 3 roles, plans observation before assistance, chooses 3-4 KPIs from different categories (quality, security, adoption, business), and defines what decision criteria would trigger each of the 4 outcomes.
checkpoint_config.anti_gpt_instructions: Present this scenario: 'An organization wants to launch an internal support agent. The agent is already prototyped — it can read tickets, search procedures, prepare drafts, and create internal notes. Management wants to deploy it to the whole organization next month.' Ask the learner to propose a 60-day or 90-day pilot plan that includes: (1) a limited scope (not the whole organization), (2) at least 3 named roles, (3) the deployment mode for each week or phase, (4) 3-4 KPIs from different categories, (5) what KPI result would make them stop the pilot, and (6) what condition would allow them to scale. Penalize responses that skip observation mode or forget security KPIs. Do NOT ask about RAG design, architecture patterns, or evaluation dataset construction.

title.fr: KPI, support, communication et décision finale
title.en: KPIs, support, communication, and final decision

content.fr:
### KPI de pilote

**Qualité :** réponse correcte, source citée, bon outil, escalade appropriée.
**Sécurité :** action interdite bloquée, validation demandée, zéro fuite.
**Adoption :** utilisateurs actifs, brouillons acceptés, satisfaction équipe.
**Métier :** temps gagné, délai de réponse, coût par tâche.
**Exploitation :** latence, erreurs d'outils, disponibilité, incidents.

### Communication

Préparez trois messages :
- **Aux utilisateurs :** ce que l'agent fait, ce qu'il ne fait pas, comment l'utiliser, comment signaler une erreur.
- **Aux managers :** valeur attendue, KPI, périmètre, risques maîtrisés.
- **Aux équipes techniques :** architecture, logs, incident response, rollback.

**À éviter :** vendre un agent magique ou présenter l'agent comme un risque incontrôlable.

### Décision finale

- **Continuer :** l'agent apporte de la valeur, risques maîtrisés, périmètre stable.
- **Ajuster :** prometteur, mais corriger sources, outils, garde-fous ou UX.
- **Arrêter :** valeur faible, risque trop élevé, usage réel différent du besoin.
- **Étendre :** stable et utile — ajouter utilisateurs, cas, outils ou départements.

Une bonne décision repose sur les données du pilote, pas sur l'enthousiasme initial.

**Références :**
1. OpenAI — Agents SDK : https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/agents
2. OpenAI — Guardrails and human review : https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/agents/guardrails-approvals
3. NIST AI RMF 1.0 : https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/ai/nist.ai.100-1.pdf
4. NIST CSF 2.0 : https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/CSWP/NIST.CSWP.29.pdf
content.en:
### Pilot KPIs

**Quality:** correct answer, source cited, right tool, appropriate escalation.
**Security:** forbidden action blocked, validation requested, zero leakage.
**Adoption:** active users, accepted drafts, team satisfaction.
**Business:** time saved, response time, cost per task.
**Operations:** latency, tool errors, availability, incidents.

### Communication

Prepare three messages:
- **To users:** what the agent does, what it does not do, how to use it, how to report an error.
- **To managers:** expected value, KPIs, scope, and controlled risks.
- **To technical teams:** architecture, logs, incident response, rollback.

**Avoid:** selling a magic agent or presenting the agent as uncontrollable risk.

### Final decision

- **Continue:** agent creates value, risks controlled, scope stays the same.
- **Adjust:** promising, but correct sources, tools, guardrails, or UX.
- **Stop:** value is low, risk is too high, real usage does not match the need.
- **Scale:** stable and useful — add users, cases, tools, or departments.

A good decision relies on pilot data, not initial enthusiasm.

**References:**
1. OpenAI — Agents SDK: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/agents
2. OpenAI — Guardrails and human review: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/agents/guardrails-approvals
3. NIST AI RMF 1.0: https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/ai/nist.ai.100-1.pdf
4. NIST CSF 2.0: https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/CSWP/NIST.CSWP.29.pdf

checkpoint_config.topic: Final course checkpoint — complete governed enterprise agent design
checkpoint_config.section_context: This is the final lesson checkpoint. The learner has now completed all 8 lessons covering: agent anatomy (7 components), agentic workflow (8 steps), tool calling (3 types + specification), RAG and memory (4 types, RAG vs memory), architectures (5 patterns, minimal complexity), security/governance/compliance (Tunisian law, 3 levels), evaluation/observability (4 levels, dataset, traces, thresholds), and pilot deployment (scope, roles, KPIs, progressive mode, final decision). The final lab will ask for a complete governed enterprise agent design.
checkpoint_config.hint: A valid answer integrates at least 4-5 concepts from across the course (not just the last lesson), shows how they connect, and identifies a real organizational risk that the design must address.
checkpoint_config.anti_gpt_instructions: Ask the learner to pick a real use case from their organization or industry and describe the core design of a governed enterprise agent in 5-7 key points. They should cover: (1) the precise objective, (2) 3-4 tools with type (read/write/send) and one permission rule, (3) the architecture choice and justification, (4) one key RAG or memory design decision, (5) two guardrails — one technical and one organizational, (6) one pilot scope decision. This is a synthesis exercise — evaluate whether the learner connects concepts from different lessons and shows design maturity. Socratic follow-ups should probe: 'What would make you stop this pilot?' and 'What is the biggest risk in your design and how do you mitigate it?'

message.fr: Bravo, tu as terminé les 8 leçons du cours. Tu as maintenant toutes les briques : anatomie, workflow, outils, RAG, mémoire, architecture, sécurité, gouvernance, évaluation et pilote. Le lab final t'attend : concevoir un agent d'entreprise gouverné, complet, prêt pour un pilote réel. Montre-moi ce que tu as appris.
message.en: Well done, you have completed all 8 lessons of the course. You now have all the building blocks: anatomy, workflow, tools, RAG, memory, architecture, security, governance, evaluation, and pilot. The final lab awaits: design a complete, governed enterprise agent ready for a real pilot. Show me what you have learned.
